questions.question_text: Ethylene glycol is added to water as antifreeze. It will
questions.question_html: Ethylene glycol is added to water as antifreeze. It will

options.text: Only increase the boiling point of water
options.html: Only increase the boiling point of water

options.text: It is used to clean the radiator in car
options.html: It is used to clean the radiator in car

options.text: Only decrease the freezing point of water
options.html: Only decrease the freezing point of water

options.text: Decrease the freezing point of water in the winter and increase the boiling point of water in the summer.
options.html: Decrease the freezing point of water in the winter and increase the boiling point of water in the summer.

questions.correct_option_text: Decrease the freezing point of water in the winter and increase the boiling point of water in the summer.
questions.explanation: Adding ethylene glycol to water reduces the freezing point of water. Water freezes at temperatures much less than 0 degrees Celsius when ethylene glycol is added to it. It is used as an anti-freeze. This practice was mostly practised in older radiators in cold weather conditions. Another aspect of using glycols is that they have a higher boiling point(197 deg C) as compared to water. Hence, they don’t get vapourised easily inside the radiator. They also have a higher specific heat capacity for which they are capable of transferring more heat from the engine as compared to water.

questions.question_html: 6.3 g oxalic acid is used to make 250 mL aqueous solution. The volume of 0.1N NaOH solution required to neutralize completely 10 mL of this solution is

questions.question_text: The depression in freezing point for 1M urea, 1 M glucose and 1 M NaCl are in the ratio of
questions.question_html: The depression in freezing point for 1M urea, 1 M glucose and 1 M NaCl are in the ratio of
questions.explanation: ΔT f = iK f ​m solute​ ΔT f1 : ΔT f2 ​ ​:ΔT f3 ​​= i 1 ​ : i 2 ​ : i 3 ​ i 1 ​ = 1 i 2 ​ = 1 i 3 ​ = 2 so ΔT f1 : ΔT f2 ​​: ΔT f3 ​​= 1:1:2

questions.question_text: 25mL of a solution of barium hydroxide on titration with 0.1molar solution of hydrochloric acid give a titre value of 35mL. The molarity of Barium hydroxide solution is
questions.question_html: 25mL of a solution of barium hydroxide on titration with 0.1molar solution of hydrochloric acid give a titre value of 35mL. The molarity of Barium hydroxide solution is
questions.explanation: M 1 v 1 = M 2 v 2 {tex}N = M \times {n_f}{/tex} {tex}35 \times 0.1 = 2 \times {M_2} \times 25{/tex} Therefore, M Ba(OH)2 = 0.07

questions.question_text: To neutralize completely 20 mL of 0.1 M aqueous solution of phosphorus acid (H 3 PO 3 ) the volume of 0.1 M aqueous KOH solution required is
questions.question_html: To neutralize completely 20 mL of 0.1 M aqueous solution of phosphorus acid (H3PO3) the volume of 0.1 M aqueous KOH solution required is
questions.explanation: H 3 PO 3 is a dibasic acid. H 3 PO 3 + 2KOH {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} K 2 HPO 3 + 2H 2 O {tex}\frac{M_{A} V_{A}}{n_{A}}=\frac{M_{B} V_{B}}{n_{B}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{0.1 \times 20}{1}=\frac{0.1 \times V_{B}}{2}{/tex} V B = 40 mL

questions.question_html: The concentration of a solution is {tex}0.3 \times {10^{ - 2}}{/tex} moles/litre. The osmotic pressure of the solution at 300K is
questions.explanation: since {tex}\pi = cRT{/tex} R = 0.0821L atmK -1 mol -1 {tex}C = 0.3 \times {10^{ - 2}}{/tex} T = 300K on substituting the value we get, {tex}= 0.3 \times {10^{ - 2}} \times0.0821 \times300{/tex} = 0.0821atm
